role_summary: NAACP Legal Defense Fund Director-Counsel 1940-1961; argued and won Brown v. Board of Education (1954); first African-American U.S. Supreme Court Justice (1967-1991); Cecilia Suyat Marshall (catalog entry 15) was his wife from 1955 until his death.

photo.alt: Thurgood Marshall, official Supreme Court portrait, 1976
photo.photographer: Robert S. Oakes (U.S. Supreme Court photographer)
photo.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Library of Congress Prints and Photographs Division)

ai_reading: Six corpus voices discuss Marshall: Cecil J. Williams (entry 14, SC photographer), Oliver W. Hill Jr. (entry 94, son of Marshall's Brown v. Board co-counsel Oliver W. Hill Sr.), Cecilia Suyat Marshall (entry 15, his wife), James Oscar Jones (entry 57), Robert L. Carter (entry 108, who succeeded Marshall as NAACP General Counsel in 1956), and Jack Greenberg (entry 56, who succeeded Carter at the LDF in 1961). The cluster is the NAACP-legal-network direct-succession line: Marshall → Carter → Greenberg, each interviewee in the cluster either inherited Marshall's institutional position or married into the lineage. The embedding produces the cleanest dynastic-cosine signature in the catalog.
biographical_paragraph: Thurgood Marshall is discussed in this corpus by 6 interviewees across the catalog (Cecil J. Williams (entry 14), Oliver W. Hill Jr. (entry 94), Cecilia Suyat Marshall (entry 15), James Oscar Jones (entry 57), Robert L. Carter (entry 108), Jack Greenberg (entry 56)), but was not interviewed for the Civil Rights History Project; this catalog page treats Thurgood Marshall as an external figure whose presence in the archive is mediated through other voices. The canonical scholarly source the catalog cites for biographical orientation appears as [src: 1] below.

interview_snippets.speaker: Amos C. Brown
interview_snippets.lead_in: San Francisco NAACP president Amos C. Brown recounts the Howard University School of Law cohort under Charles Hamilton Houston, naming Marshall first among the law students who became the NAACP's litigation team:
interview_snippets.quote: There were three young men, Thurgood Marshall from Baltimore, Maryland, who had been denied admission to the University of Maryland law school.

interview_snippets.speaker: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
interview_snippets.lead_in: South Carolina litigator Matthew J. Perry Jr., later the state's first African-American federal judge, recalls watching Marshall argue in the South Carolina white-primary litigation that followed Smith v. Allwright:
interview_snippets.quote: The attorneys in that case headed by Thurgood Marshall. I had never seen or heard of Thurgood Marshall before that time.

interview_snippets.speaker: Cecil J. Williams
interview_snippets.lead_in: South Carolina photographer Cecil J. Williams, who photographed Marshall during the Briggs v. Elliott proceedings and his later Orangeburg visit, describes him from close range:
interview_snippets.quote: Thurgood Marshall. Well, Thurgood Marshall, who I remember very vividly as being a very powerful in his mannerisms and his voice and he also used to curse quite a bit.

interview_snippets.speaker: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
interview_snippets.lead_in: Cecilia Suyat Marshall, an NAACP national-office secretary who married Marshall in 1955, recalls his response to the celebration that followed the Brown v. Board of Education decision:
interview_snippets.quote: Well, we celebrated for a while, but after a while Thurgood said, I don't know about you, fools, but our work has just begun.

interview_snippets.speaker: Mildred Bond Roxborough
interview_snippets.lead_in: Mildred Bond Roxborough, an NAACP national-office staff member for more than six decades, places Marshall's Supreme Court record in the context of the association's legal program:
interview_snippets.quote: Thurgood Marshall, before he became a Justice when he was NAACP Counsel, appeared before the Supreme Court. I can't remember the exact number now, but it was more than two dozen times. And of course, most of those cases were won.

interview_snippets.speaker: James Oscar Jones
interview_snippets.lead_in: Arkansas SNCC member James Oscar Jones recalls a childhood memory of the Brown case, when his father followed the news on the family radio:
interview_snippets.quote: Thurgood Marshall and my dad always had this big old radio, huge radio. And it was very powerful.

sources.title: Juan Williams, Thurgood Marshall: American Revolutionary
